Structural proof theory is the subdiscipline of proof theory that studies the specifics of proof calculi
a proof calculus has the cut-elimination property if it has the cut rule, but any provable sequent is provable without the cut rule

annotations.label: Corollaries of cut-elimination (midsequent, Craig interpolation, Herbrand)

anchor.snippet: Gentzen's midsequent theorem , the Craig interpolation theorem , and Herbrand's theorem follow as corollaries of the cut-elimination property
A proof has the subformula property if every of its formula is a subformula of its endsequent
A proof calculus with the subformula property is consistent

A maximal formula is a formula that is introduced, then eliminated later

Structural proof theory is connected to type theory by means of the Curry–Howard correspondence
Ordinal analysis is a powerful technique for providing combinatorial consistency proofs for subsystems of arithmetic, analysis, and set theory

Ordinal analysis was originated by Gentzen, who proved the consistency of Peano Arithmetic using transfinite induction

Provability logic is a modal logic , in which the box operator is interpreted as 'it is provable that'
Löb's theorem (if it is provable that the provability of A implies A, then A is provable)
Robert Solovay proved that the modal logic GL is complete with respect to Peano Arithmetic
Reverse mathematics is a program in mathematical logic that seeks to determine which axioms are required to prove theorems of mathematics

Functional interpretations are interpretations of non-constructive theories in functional ones
The study of functional interpretations began with Kurt Gödel's interpretation of intuitionistic arithmetic in a quantifier-free theory of functionals of finite type
